briefdescription.para: The static initializer for struct can_buf.

parameterdescription.para: a pointer to a CAN frame buffer.

a pointer to the memory region to be used by the buffer. If not NULL, the buffer takes ownership of the region at ptr and MAY reallocate or free the region during subsequent calls to can_buf_reserve() and canbuf_fini().
the number of frames available at ptr. If not 0, size MUST be a power of two.

briefdescription.para: Returns the number of frames available for reading in a CAN buffer.
Returns the number of frames available for writing in a CAN buffer.
Resizes a CAN frame buffer, if necessary, to make room for at least n additional frames.

detaileddescription.para: Note that the new capacity can be larger than the requested capacity.

simplesect.para: the new capacity of the buffer (in number of frames), or 0 on error. In the latter case, the error code can be obtained with get_errc().

memberdef.argsstring: (const struct can_buf *buf, struct can_msg *ptr, size_t n)
memberdef.name: can_buf_peek

briefdescription.para: Reads, but does not remove, frames from a CAN frame buffer.

parameterdescription.para: a pointer to a CAN frame buffer.
the address at which to store the frames (can be NULL).
the number of frames to read.

briefdescription.para: Reads, and removes, frames from a CAN frame buffer.

parameterdescription.para: a pointer to a CAN frame buffer.
the address at which to store the frames (can be NULL).
the number of frames to read.

memberdef.argsstring: (struct can_buf *buf, const struct can_msg *ptr, size_t n)
memberdef.name: can_buf_write

briefdescription.para: Writes frames to a CAN frame buffer.

parameterdescription.para: a pointer to a CAN frame buffer.
the address from which to load the frames.
the number of frames to write.

briefdescription.para: This header file is part of the CAN library; it contains the CAN frame buffer declarations.

detaileddescription.para: The CAN frame buffer is implemented as a circular buffer. Apart from can_buf_clear() and can_buf_reserve() (an obviously can_buf_init() and can_buf_fini()), all buffer functions are thread-safe and lock-free, provided there are at most two threads accessing the buffer at the same time (single-reader single-writer).
